lesson.menu_desc: Close Block 6 and the program: run a coaching conversation that grows someone between reviews. Lead with open coaching questions instead of giving answers ("What would success look like?", "What is one step you could take this week?"), and use the zero conditional to map cause and effect in a growth plan ("If you block two hours a day, the deep work gets done"). New vocab: coaching, mentor, potential, growth mindset, stretch goal, ownership, reflect, breakthrough, empower, unlock, self-aware, roadblock — 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 — Coaching & Development Conversations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Victor Malvezi Paschotto | Aula 20 — Coaching & Development Conversations

listenings.text: Prep note to myself, the night before my first real coaching conversation with Sofia, between the formal reviews. The review told her where she stood. This is different. Tonight I am not here to judge or to hand her answers, I am here to help her find her own. So the rule I keep reminding myself is simple: ask, do not tell. If I tell her what to do, she does my plan. If I ask the right questions, she owns hers. So I want to lead with open questions, the kind she cannot answer with a yes or a no. What would success look like in six months? What is getting in the way right now? What is one step you could take this week? My job is to listen, reflect back what I hear, and let the silence do some of the work, even when it feels uncomfortable. The second thing I want to get right is the growth plan itself, and there I am thinking in cause and effect. If she blocks two hours each morning for deep work, the dashboard gets done on time. If she shadows a senior analyst once a week, she builds the modeling skill faster. That is the logic I want her to see, not because I dictate it, but because we map it together. She has real potential, more than she gives herself credit for, and a stretch goal would unlock it if she takes ownership of the path. One last reminder to myself: a coach does not rescue. If I jump in with the answer the moment she pauses, I take the growth away from her. Hold the question. Let her think. That is the whole job tonight.

Hi Victor, it is Clara again, your coach, with a voice note before you sit down to coach Sofia. You have run the review, so now comes the harder, quieter skill: coaching. Three things. First, and this is everything, ask instead of tell. A manager gives answers, a coach asks questions, and the questions have to be open. Closed questions get you a yes or a no and the conversation dies. Open questions, the what and the how, get her thinking out loud. Try What would a great quarter look like for you, or How will you know you are making progress. The moment you hear yourself starting a sentence with You should, stop, and turn it into a question. Second, build the plan as cause and effect, and the grammar that carries that is the zero conditional. Not a maybe, a general truth. If she protects her mornings, the deep work gets done. If she asks for feedback early, the surprises disappear. Notice the shape: if plus present, then present. It is the language of how things reliably work, and it makes a growth plan feel like a system, not a wish. Third, let her own it. Your job is to empower her, not to rescue her, so resist filling every silence. When she pauses, she is thinking, and that thinking is the breakthrough. A self-aware person with a growth mindset does not need you to have the answer. She needs you to hold the space and ask one more good question. Coach the person, not the problem. You have got this, and Victor, congratulations, this is your last session in the program. Go finish strong.
